titleStmt.title: 216. Memorandum From Helmut Sonnenfeldt of the National Security Council Staff to the Presidentʼs Assistant for National Security Affairs ( of the National Security Council Staff to the Presidentʼs Assistant for National Security Affairs (Kissinger))

div.head: 216. Memorandum From Helmut Sonnenfeldt of the National Security Council Staff to the Presidentʼs Assistant for National Security Affairs (Kissinger)Source: National Archives, Nixon Presidential Materials, NSC Files, Box 733, Country Files—Europe, Yugoslavia, Vol. I through Jul 70. Secret; Nodis. Sent for action.

list.head: SUBJECT
list.item: Holding Yugoslav Hands in Connection with Presidentʼs Stop in Bucharest

div.p: In view of past correspondence and conversations, I think we need to say something to the Yugoslavs before the Presidentʼs Romanian tripThe trip was announced on June 28; see Documents 183 and 184. is publicly announced. Tito will undoubtedly feel let down.
In the event you wish to run this approach past the President (which I personally would not think necessary), there is attached a memorandum for your signature at Tab A.Attached but not printed.
1. That you (or I) approach the Yugoslavs shortly before the announcement of the Presidentʼs trip to make the points set forth above.
Joan set up appointment with Yugoslav Ambassador
Sonnenfeldt handle with DCM. Yes Kissinger initialed this option. No

2. That, if you first want to check with the President, you sign the attached memorandum. Kissinger did not sign the attached memorandum. In telegram 1919 from Belgrade, July 4, the Embassy reported that Yugoslav senior officials “thoroughly endorsed” the Presidentʼs trip and its objectives. (National Archives, RG 59, Central Files 1967–69, POL 15–1 YUGO)
